title: Your First Component - Function Basics
description: Create your first React functional component and understand component syntax.
longDescription: Components are the building blocks of React. In this lesson, you'll write your very first functional component, learn the syntax rules, and understand how components differ from regular functions. By the end, you'll be creating reusable UI pieces like a pro!

learningObjectives.statement: Define what a React component is
Create a functional component using correct syntax
Distinguish between components and regular functions

missionNarrative.title: The Component Workshop
missionNarrative.story: Welcome to the Component Workshop! Here, master builders craft reusable UI pieces that power entire applications. Your first task? Build your very own component from scratch. Don't worry - Professor Syntax is here to guide you!

sections.title: What is a Component?
sections.content:
A **React component** is a JavaScript function that returns JSX (UI markup). Think of it as a custom HTML element that you define yourself.

**Key characteristics:**
- Starts with a **capital letter** (e.g., `MyComponent`)
- Returns **JSX** (looks like HTML but lives in JavaScript)
- Can be **reused** throughout your app
- Manages its own **structure** and eventually its own **state**

Components let you break complex UIs into smaller, manageable pieces!

sections.title: Your First Component
Important Rule!
sections.content:
**Component names MUST start with a capital letter!** ```javascript function greeting() { return

Hi

; } // ❌ Not a component! function Greeting() { return

Hi

; } // ✅ This is a component! ``` React uses the capital letter to distinguish between custom components and built-in HTML elements.

sections.title: Component Rules
More Component Examples
The Power of Reusability

challenges.title: Spot the Component
challenges.description: Identify which functions are valid React components
challenges.question: Which of these are valid React components?

options.explanation: Correct! Capital letter, returns JSX - perfect component!
Wrong! Component names must start with a capital letter.
Correct! Arrow function with capital name returning JSX works!
Nope! This doesn't return any JSX.
Correct! Implicit return with JSX is valid!

challenges.successFeedback: Excellent! You know how to spot a valid component!
challenges.failureFeedback: Remember: capital letters and returning JSX are essential!

hints.text: Check if the function name starts with a capital letter.
Make sure the function returns JSX markup.
Arrow functions can implicitly return JSX without braces!

hints.finalExplanation: Valid components need: 1) Capital letter name, 2) Return JSX. Both function declarations and arrow functions work!

challenges.title: Fix the Component
challenges.description: Debug and fix broken component code
challenges.question: Fix the errors in this component
challenges.instructions: Fill in the blanks to make this a valid React component.
challenges.failureFeedback: Check that all tags are properly closed and the structure is valid.

hints.text: Component names should be descriptive and start with a capital letter.
Every opening tag needs a matching closing tag.

challenges.title: Build a Component
challenges.description: Create a simple component from scratch
challenges.question: Create a Footer component
challenges.instructions: Complete the code to create a Footer component that returns a <footer> element with a paragraph containing copyright text.

testCases.description: Component returns JSX with footer element

challenges.successFeedback: Awesome! You've created your own reusable component!
challenges.failureFeedback: Make sure your component has a capital letter name and returns JSX.

hints.text: Start with 'function Footer()' and use curly braces.
Use 'return' followed by your JSX in parentheses.
Don't forget to close all your tags!

reflectionQuestion.question: Think about a website you visit often. What are 3 components you could extract from it? Name each component and describe what it would contain.
reflectionQuestion.sampleAnswer: For YouTube: 1) VideoPlayer - the main video player with controls. 2) VideoCard - thumbnail, title, and channel info for each video. 3) CommentSection - list of comments with reply functionality.
